other_side: NLW reads Claude Tag, and the move "from prompting to delegation," as a genuine third redesign of how we work with AI — a paradigm shift, not just a feature. Writing the day before, Zitron reads the very same "the age of prompting is over, now you just handle loops" framing as a desperate, self-interested push to get users to burn more tokens for revenue, noting Jensen Huang and Claude Code's Boris Cherny floated it within twenty-four hours of each other. He points out that Cherny is, in his words, "allowed to burn upwards of $130,000 a month in tokens" and runs agents that never stop running. More broadly, he casts enterprise AI adoption itself as a cargo cult: executives "demanding that people use it because 'AI is so powerful'" while having little idea what it actually does, copying whatever everyone else is doing rather than what is demonstrably valuable.
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether the shift from prompting to autonomous delegation creates real, durable productivity or merely consumes more tokens. Both accept that agents and loops exist, that they burn compute, and that Anthropic genuinely leans on its internal tool. The empirical question that will resolve it is whether delegated AI yields measurable output net of its true cost; the observable that settles it is audited productivity and retention once access is charged at unsubsidised prices rather than on free credits. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore sees a paradigm shift, Zitron a revenue-driven ritual. Whittemore is vindicated if enterprises show sustained, measured gains at real prices; Zitron if usage and the headline "sixty-five percent of code" figures collapse when subsidies end. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain and anti-hype with a paid newsletter — and the signal is their shared doubt that the average eleven-and-a-half-million-dollar enterprise AI spend can prove a dollar back. Watch whether ROI appears once the free credits stop.
